About this site

About Digital Minds

What this is, how to read it, and a note on sources.

Digital Minds is a reader’s pathway through the history of thinking about the mind, from Plato’s ψυχή to contemporary philosophy of mind, consciousness science, and the ethics of artificial minds. It is a personal research project by Fabián, compiled out of a long-standing interest in how the questions that now define philosophy of mind developed out of ancient, medieval, and modern reflection on the soul.

The site does not assume prior training in philosophy, but it also does not try to simplify the material into executive summaries. Each thinker’s page is a serious, primary-source-forward essay — the kind of treatment one might read in a careful scholarly encyclopedia — aimed at a curious reader willing to follow an argument. The essays make heavy use of the philosophers’ own texts rather than relying on modern commentary, and they try to hold the voice of each author steady rather than forcing them into a uniform system.

How the site is organized

The material is laid out both chronologically and by tradition. The landing page presents a short visual timeline. From there, era pages — Antiquity, Late Antiquity, the Islamic Tradition, the Jewish Tradition, Medieval Latin Scholasticism, South Asian Traditions, the Renaissance, Early Modern thought, the Nineteenth Century, the Phenomenological Tradition, Analytic Philosophy of Mind, Consciousness Science, Machine Minds, and the ethics of Moral Status — each frame a period or a school and link to the individual thinkers.

Each philosopher’s page stands on its own. A glossary provides working definitions for the technical vocabulary of contemporary philosophy of mind — qualia, emergence, causal closure, multiple realizability, and so on — for readers new to the field.

A note on sources

The philosopher essays are primary-source-oriented: each draws directly on the extant texts of the philosopher in question, using translations where the original languages are inaccessible. The essays try to minimize reliance on secondary commentary and to stay close to what the texts actually say. Where a tradition has multiple schools or interpretations — as with Plato’s tripartite soul, Averroes’s unified intellect, or Chalmers’s naturalistic dualism — the essays attempt to present the strongest form of the author’s argument and flag major interpretive disputes rather than adjudicating them.

The glossary entries are informed by standard reference works including the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, the Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy, and Britannica.

A note on method

The working premise of the project is that philosophical questions do not become obsolete by being rephrased. The ancient and medieval traditions were not working on different questions that have since been superseded; they were working on the same questions in a different idiom. Reading them seriously is one of the best ways to see what is and is not new about the contemporary debate. The aim is not to endorse any particular metaphysics or to dismiss any other, but to let each thinker speak with the precision they actually had — and then to listen to what our current questions sound like against that background.

Errors and infelicities are, of course, the author’s own. Suggestions, corrections, and serious disagreements are welcome.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
